title: Lecsó
subtitle: Hungarian pepper and tomato stew
excerpt: A thick, paprika-red stew of sweet peppers, tomatoes, and onion cooked down to a jammy, yielding mass. Eaten as a side dish, a sauce, or with sliced sausage stirred through and eggs broken on top.
sourceNotes: Lecsó is the Hungarian interpretation of the pepper-and-tomato stew found across Central and Eastern Europe (ratatouille in France, pipérade in the Basque country). Documented in George Lang, The Cuisine of Hungary (1971) and Lesley Chamberlain, The Food and Cooking of Eastern Europe (1989).

recipe.freezeNotes: Freeze for up to 3 months. Defrosts well.
recipe.batchNotes: Make a large batch in summer when peppers are cheap.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Improves overnight. Reheat and add the eggs fresh.

content.text: Lecsó is what you make when there are more peppers and tomatoes than you know what to do with. The vegetables cook down slowly in lard with paprika until they collapse into a thick, yielding stew — sweeter than ratatouille, more paprika-forward than pipérade.

The simplest version is just peppers, tomatoes, onion, and paprika. The more common version adds sliced smoked sausage (csabai or other kielbasa), which turns the whole thing into a substantial one-pan dinner. If eggs go in (two or three cracked into the lecsó in the last 5 minutes and stirred through gently) it becomes a Hungarian shakshuka, filling and complete.

items.prepNote: optional — stir in at the end

content.text: Cook the onion and build the base

Melt the lard in a large saucepan over medium heat. Add the onion and a pinch of salt and cook for 10 minutes until softened. Remove from the heat, add the paprika, and stir. Return to the heat. Add the garlic and cook for 1 minute.

Add the peppers and cook, stirring occasionally, for 10 minutes until they begin to soften. Add the tomatoes and cook for a further 15 minutes until the mixture is thick and jammy. If using sausage, add it with the peppers. Season with salt and pepper. For the egg version, make small hollows in the stew, crack in the eggs, and stir gently through the warm lecsó until just set. Serve with bread.

items.cause: Too much liquid from the tomatoes; needs more cooking time
items.fix: Cook uncovered over medium heat, stirring often, until the moisture evaporates and the stew thickens

items.cause: Good-quality sweet Hungarian paprika is essential; cheap supermarket paprika gives a flat, sometimes bitter result
items.fix: Use genuine Hungarian paprika — it makes a perceptible difference in this dish where paprika is the primary spice

content.text: Lecsó is summer food in Hungary — made when peppers come in quantities and cost almost nothing. The jar of lecsó (preserved in a waterbath for winter use) is as much a Hungarian pantry staple as tinned tomatoes are elsewhere. The simplest version is just a side dish; with sausage and egg it becomes a full meal, eaten with bread torn into the pan.
